body_txt:
Just because you're officially in charge, that doesn't mean you're the one actually in charge. The Dragon-in-Chief is someone who serves as the de facto Big Bad of the story, but are not technically the top of the bad guy hierarchy. In standard practice they are The Dragon , nominally subordinate or in service to another villain, but are so much smarter, stronger and skillful that they serve as not just the biggest threat to the heroes but whatever Evil Plan is in motion hinges on their capabilities. As a result they often come across as more evil than their alleged boss, and almost always scarier . They tend to have little respect for the nominal Big Bad due to their comparative lack of vision , courage or common sense . The supposed main villain, for their part, is often a Big Bad Wannabe as they fatally overestimate The Dragon 's loyalty or underestimate the gap in power . Usually the boss is too afraid to keep this particular servant in line, even j
If they're a Bad Boss to everyone else. It could also be that the Big Bad is more of a Puppet King being manipulated by subordinates, and the Dragon finds this set-up preferable to taking charge personally. The Dragon-in-Chief is not simply the main villainous driving force behind the plot , even if they did not initiate it, but if the villainous organization pushes them aside the entire operation is rendered less competent . In fact, a good early litmus test of a Dragon-in-Chief is whether the Big Bad is a threat to the protagonists without said Dragon's backing, or if it's the other way around. Because they represent the biggest physical and intellectual threat of the story, The Hero usually regards the Dragon-In-Chief as the antagonist with the Big Bad as a footnote, and very often It's Personal with the Dragon . The Dragon-In-Chief will typically think the Big Bad either lacks ambition, is a coward, or is just an idiot . They may start off as junior partners in the Big Bad's business: after years of hard (but fun) living as a dangerous felon, they have found themselves steady employment with the Big Bad and hope to take over the business some day or retire on the fortune made from their latest Master Plan . This is when they start to complain about their master's unambitious and/or just plain incompetent way of running things , though the Big Bad might retort that their way is from experience and The Dragon's ways will ultimately lead to ruin. Occasionally, their warnings turn out to be right . Alternatively, the Dragon-in-Chief may be a speechless giant or god-like character opposing another heroic character in its weight class. The Big Bad will usually act as a Mouth of Sauron while the giants duke it out, If the evil "giant" is treated as the main threat, then it's this trope, as without them the Big Bad isn't a factor. If it's treated as the Big Bad 's disposable Monster of the Week , then it's a regular Dragon. The Man in Front of the Man is a related, but different trope where the position of The Dragon as the actual Big Bad is kept hidden until The Reveal , thus The Dragon is less likely to act like a true Dragon-in-Chief in order to keep the jig up from the audience as well as the in-universe characters, including the supposed Big Bad , who might not be aware that they are being manipulated . The Dragon-in-Chief doesn't need this theatre, making their position as the true Big Bad clear from the start. When a character fills this role because the Big Bad is merely physically absent from the main story, and/or not as important to the hero, then the superior is a Greater-Scope Villain (if the Dragon is operating entirely or almost entirely on their own). Rarely, the Dragon-In-Chief truly is loyal to the current Big Bad , but the plot is built around the former as the true threat anyway. If it is genuine mutual loyalty, the Villainous Friendship between the two may be one of their most dangerous assets—a deadly inverted version of The Power of Friendship . In occasions like these, unless it develops into an equal partnership , the less relevant superior is still liable to be shunted aside by the plot to allow the Dragon-in-Chief a proper climatic throwdown of some type. If the Big Bad outlives the Dragon-in-Chief , they're either quickly disposed of afterwards in a Post-Climax Confrontation , or saved for a Sequel Hook focusing on them, but the Dragon-in-Chief remained the main threat of the preceding plot. See also Hyper-Competent Sidekick , Dragon Ascendant , The Starscream and especially the Big Bad Wannabe , whom Dragons-in-Chief usually work for. Compare/Contrast The Heavy , which often overlaps with The Dragon and refers to the villain with the most plot importance as opposed to the most powerful and threatening one. Also compare Big Bad Duumvirate , if the apparent Dragon-in-Chief and lesser Big Bad are treated as a combined threat by the narrative, but there's occasional overlap in episodic works. Important Note : this does not simply refer to any Dragon that is physically superior to a Non-Action Big Bad or Greater-Scope Villain who can be dispatched with relative ease. Plenty of physically weaker villains present far greater moral, psychological or other challenges to the hero, and their actions drive the character arcs of the protagonists. note Darth Vader , for example, may be the main physical threat, but Emperor Palpatine is undoubtedly the one in control, and could replace Vader with someone else without undermining his own threat level. It is the story importance, not simply power level, that must be supplanted for a Dragon to rise to Dragon-in-Chief. Conversely, a Dragon-in-Chief can sometimes be outlasted by their "lesser" boss, but still qualify for a specific installment or Story Arc . Please be sure the character actually fits this criteria before adding them as an example. noreallife
